function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC

results.content:
# Connectivity Segment Capital Expenditures

## Exact Figures

| Period | Connectivity Capex |
|---|---|
| **Q1 2026** (three months ended March 31, 2026) | **$1,332 million** |
| **FY2025** (year ended December 31, 2025) | **$4,178 million** |

## Context from the Filing

**Year-over-year growth drivers:** The filing attributes the increases in both periods to the same underlying cause:

> "The increase in each year-over-year period was primarily driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network."

**Comparative trajectory:**
- Q1 2026 capex of $1,332 million represented a **$518 million increase** over Q1 2025's $814 million
- FY2025 capex of $4,178 million represented a **$680 million increase** over FY2024's $3,498 million
- FY2024 had itself increased $1,043 million over FY2023's $2,455 million

**Segment performance context:** The rising capex coincided with strong Connectivity segment growth in 2025, which generated revenue of $11,387 million (up 49.8% year-over-year), income from operations of $4,423 million (up 120.4%), and Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million (up 86.2%), benefiting from subscriber growth, increasing enterprise adoption, and continued improvement in network efficiency.

results.content:
Based on the provided text chunks from Amazon's 10-K filing, here are the relevant disclosures and the line items an analyst would use to triangulate Kuiper investment:

### 1. Disclosures About Project Kuiper / Satellite Investments
The filing mentions satellite investments within the definition of **Technology and infrastructure costs**:
> "Technology and infrastructure costs include payroll and related expenses for employees involved in the research and development of new and existing products and services... Collectively, these costs reflect the investments we make in order to offer a wide variety of products and services to our customers, including expenditures related to initiatives to build and deploy innovative and efficient software and electronic devices and **the development of a satellite network for global broadband service** and autonomous vehicles for ride-hailing services."

### 2. "Purchases of property and equipment" in the Cash Flow Statement
This line item is found in the Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows under Investing Activities:
> **Purchases of property and equipment** | $(52,729) | | $(82,999) | | $(131,819) | (for years 2023, 2024, and 2025, respectively)

It is also referenced in the MD&A as:
> **Purchases of property and equipment, net of proceeds from sales and incentives**

### 3. Segment Reporting Disclosures
The filing defines its reporting structure in the significant accounting policies note:
> "We have organized our operations into **three segments: North America, International, and AWS**. See “Note 10 — Segment Information.”"

The XBRL metadata also confirms these segment classifications: `amzn:NorthAmericaSegmentMember`, `amzn:InternationalSegmentMember`, and `amzn:AmazonWebServicesSegmentMember`.

---

### Specific Line Items to Triangulate Kuiper Investment
Because Amazon does not explicitly break out "Project Kuiper" as a separate financial line item, an analyst would need to triangulate the investment by using the following components:

1. **Technology and Infrastructure Costs (Operating Expense):** This is the primary line item capturing the R&D payroll and infrastructure costs (servers, networking, data center depreciation) explicitly tied to the satellite network development.
2. **Purchases of Property and Equipment (Investing Cash Flow):** This captures the capital expenditures (CapEx) associated with building the satellite network. An analyst would look at the total CapEx and attempt to isolate the portion attributable to Kuiper.
3. **Property and Equipment Sub-Categories (Balance Sheet):** Using the XBRL tags provided (`amzn:ServersAndNetworkingEquipmentMember`, `us-gaap:ConstructionInProgressMember`, `us-gaap:OtherCapitalizedPropertyPlantAndEquipmentMember`), an analyst can track specific asset classes where Kuiper-related capital is likely being capitalized.
4. **Segment Information (Note 10):** An analyst would examine the segment breakdown (North America, International, AWS) to see if Kuiper costs are being allocated to a specific segment (e.g., AWS for cloud/network services or International for global broadband) or if they are buried in corporate/unallocated expenses.

**Satellite Network Scale and Deployment Stage**
*   **Satellites in Orbit:** As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX operates approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO). *   **Current Capacity:** The current constellation supports over 700 Tbps of cumulative downlink capacity.

**Maturity of the Satellite Network & Next-Stage Deployment**
While the network is described as the "world’s largest and most advanced space-based internet broadband service," it is explicitly in a rapid scaling and transition phase rather than a mature, static state:
*   **V3 Satellite Transition:** The company expects to commence deploying next-generation V3 satellites in the second half of 2026. These are designed to offer 1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite.
*   **Starship Integration:** SpaceX expects a single Starship launch to deploy up to 60 V3 satellites (or approximately 50 mobile satellites), representing a twenty-fold increase in downlink capacity deployed per launch compared to Falcon 9. This indicates the network is entering a new deployment phase that will "materially increase the capacity" of the constellation at a significantly lower cost.

**Subscriber Metrics and Revenue**
*   **Subscriber Count:** As of March 31, 2026, the network provides connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets. This is up approximately 105% from 5.0 million subscribers a year prior. 
*   **Mobile Users:** The segment also serves approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices with satellite-to-mobile texting and voice services.
*   **Revenue:** For the three months ended March 31, 2026, the Connectivity segment generated revenue of $3,257 million. For the full year 2025, it generated $11,387 million, representing a 49.8% year-over-year growth.

**Connectivity Segment Capex vs. Historical Periods**
The company is in a heavy infrastructure investment stage for the Connectivity segment, with capital expenditures increasing year-over-year to fund satellite and ground equipment expansion:

arguments.final_result:
## Assessment: SpaceX Connectivity Capex vs. Amazon's Project Kuiper Commitment

### 1. Disclosed SpaceX Connectivity Segment Capex (from S-1)

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) discloses the following Connectivity segment capital expenditures:

| Period | Connectivity Capex |
|---|---|
| FY2023 | $2,455 million |
| FY2024 | $3,498 million |
| **FY2025** | **$4,178 million** |
| **Q1 2026** | **$1,332 million** |
| Q1 2025 (comparator) | $814 million |

The filing attributes the year-over-year increases to "higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network."

**Cumulative context (calculated):**
- FY2023–FY2025 cumulative Connectivity capex = $2,455M + $3,498M + $4,178M = **$10,131 million (~$10.1 billion)**
- FY2023–Q1 2026 cumulative = ~$11.5 billion
- Q1 2026 annualized run-rate = $1,332M × 4 = **~$5.3 billion**

### 2. Amazon's Stated Commitment

Amazon publicly committed "more than $10 billion" to Project Kuiper in July 2020 (at FCC authorization). Industry analysts (per the question) estimate total program cost at $16.5–20 billion. Amazon does **not** separately disclose Kuiper capex in its 10-K segment reporting, which is organized into only three segments: North America, International, and AWS.

### 3. What the Two Figures Suggest About Relative Deployment Stage

**SpaceX is at a mature, revenue-generating scaling stage.** As of March 31, 2026, the S-1 discloses:
- ~9,600 Starlink satellites in LEO (SpaceX owns ~75% of all active maneuverable satellites globally)
- ~10.3 million Starlink subscribers across 164 markets (up ~105% YoY)
- FY2025 Connectivity revenue of $11,387 million (+49.8% YoY)
- FY2025 segment income from operations of $4,423 million (+120.4%)
- FY2025 Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million (+86.2%)
- >700 Tbps cumulative downlink capacity
- Next-generation V3 satellites and Starship integration expected H2 2026

SpaceX's $4.178 billion FY2025 Connectivity capex therefore represents **ongoing capacity expansion and next-generation technology refresh of an already-operational, cash-generative constellation** — not initial build-out.

**Amazon is at an early deployment stage.** Project Kuiper's first operational satellites launched only on April 28, 2025 (27 satellites via ULA Atlas V). As of November 2025, Amazon had conducted approximately six successful launches. The program was rebranded "Amazon Leo" in November 2025, signaling transition from experimental to commercial deployment. The FCC requires 50% of the 3,236-satellite constellation by July 2026 and full deployment by July 2029. Amazon's $10 billion figure is a **multi-year program commitment announced in 2020**, not annual spending.

**Key inference from the comparison:** SpaceX's cumulative Connectivity capex over just FY2023–FY2025 (~$10.1 billion) already equals Amazon's entire multi-year $10 billion commitment floor — and SpaceX's single-year FY2025 capex ($4.178 billion) represents ~42% of Amazon's total program commitment. This indicates SpaceX has deployed capital at a scale and cadence that Amazon has only committed to over the full life of its program, consistent with SpaceX's multi-year operational head start and Amazon's nascent deployment phase.

### 4. Analytical Limitation: Segment-Level Disclosure vs. Program-Level Commitment

Comparing these two figures presents a fundamental **apples-to-oranges** problem along several dimensions:

| Dimension | SpaceX (S-1) | Amazon (10-K / public statements) |
|---|---|---|
| **Disclosure type** | Segment-level capex (actual cash spent) | Program-level commitment (stated intention) |
| **Time basis** | Flow — annual/quarterly spending in a defined period | Stock — cumulative multi-year commitment (announced 2020) |
| **Scope** | Connectivity segment only (Starlink + mobile) | Project Kuiper only (but not isolated in financials) |
| **Accounting treatment** | Capitalized as PP&E within segment | Mostly expensed to "Technology and infrastructure" until commercial viability; launch deposits in prepaid/other assets |
| **Verifiability** | Audited segment financial disclosure | Management statement / analyst estimate; not separately quantified in 10-K |
| **Maturity adjustment** | Reflects mature, revenue-generating network | Reflects pre-commercial build-out phase |

## Assessment: SpaceX Connectivity Capex vs. Amazon's Project Kuiper Commitment

### 1. Disclosed SpaceX Connectivity Segment Capex (from S-1)

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) discloses the following Connectivity segment capital expenditures:

| Period | Connectivity Capex |
|---|---|
| FY2023 | $2,455 million |
| FY2024 | $3,498 million |
| **FY2025** | **$4,178 million** |
| **Q1 2026** | **$1,332 million** |
| Q1 2025 (comparator) | $814 million |

The filing attributes the year-over-year increases to "higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network."

**Cumulative context (calculated):**
- FY2023–FY2025 cumulative Connectivity capex = $2,455M + $3,498M + $4,178M = **$10,131 million (~$10.1 billion)**
- FY2023–Q1 2026 cumulative = ~$11.5 billion
- Q1 2026 annualized run-rate = $1,332M × 4 = **~$5.3 billion**

### 2. Amazon's Stated Commitment

Amazon publicly committed "more than $10 billion" to Project Kuiper in July 2020 (at FCC authorization). Industry analysts (per the question) estimate total program cost at $16.5–20 billion. Amazon does **not** separately disclose Kuiper capex in its 10-K segment reporting, which is organized into only three segments: North America, International, and AWS.

### 3. What the Two Figures Suggest About Relative Deployment Stage

**SpaceX is at a mature, revenue-generating scaling stage.** As of March 31, 2026, the S-1 discloses:
- ~9,600 Starlink satellites in LEO (SpaceX owns ~75% of all active maneuverable satellites globally)
- ~10.3 million Starlink subscribers across 164 markets (up ~105% YoY)
- FY2025 Connectivity revenue of $11,387 million (+49.8% YoY)
- FY2025 segment income from operations of $4,423 million (+120.4%)
- FY2025 Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million (+86.2%)
- >700 Tbps cumulative downlink capacity
- Next-generation V3 satellites and Starship integration expected H2 2026

SpaceX's $4.178 billion FY2025 Connectivity capex therefore represents **ongoing capacity expansion and next-generation technology refresh of an already-operational, cash-generative constellation** — not initial build-out.

**Amazon is at an early deployment stage.** Project Kuiper's first operational satellites launched only on April 28, 2025 (27 satellites via ULA Atlas V). As of November 2025, Amazon had conducted approximately six successful launches. The program was rebranded "Amazon Leo" in November 2025, signaling transition from experimental to commercial deployment. The FCC requires 50% of the 3,236-satellite constellation by July 2026 and full deployment by July 2029. Amazon's $10 billion figure is a **multi-year program commitment announced in 2020**, not annual spending.

**Key inference from the comparison:** SpaceX's cumulative Connectivity capex over just FY2023–FY2025 (~$10.1 billion) already equals Amazon's entire multi-year $10 billion commitment floor — and SpaceX's single-year FY2025 capex ($4.178 billion) represents ~42% of Amazon's total program commitment.
